probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and Brier score separately for each of the 6 ethnicity one-hot groups. Assess equalized odds (TPR/FPR gap), demographic parity difference, and predictive parity across groups. A healthy signal: similar discrimination metrics across subgroups (max gap < 0.05 AUROC, TPR gap < 0.10). Unhealthy: disparities >0.10 AUROC or systematic under-prediction for minority groups (e.g., Black, Hispanic). Use bootstrap resampling for confidence intervals to ensure differences are statistically significant rather than artifacts of small subgroup size.
possible_sources: Chen et al. 'Ethical Machine Learning in Healthcare' Annual Review 2021
Obermeyer et al. 'Dissecting racial bias in an algorithm used to manage the health of populations' Science 2019
Hardt et al. 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning' NeurIPS 2016
MIMIC-III benchmark fairness analyses (Röösli et al. 2022)

probe_designs.content: Plot reliability diagrams and compute Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Maximum Calibration Error (MCE) overall and per ethnicity subgroup. Mortality probability outputs should match observed event rates per bin. Healthy: ECE < 0.05 across all groups, slope ~1 and intercept ~0 in calibration regression. Unhealthy: systematic over- or under-estimation in specific subgroups (e.g., overconfident high-risk predictions for one ethnicity). Apply Hosmer-Lemeshow test per group; differential miscalibration indicates fairness-relevant degradation distinct from discrimination disparity.
possible_sources: Guo et al. 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks' ICML 2017
Pleiss et al. 'On Fairness and Calibration' NeurIPS 2017
Barda et al. 'Addressing bias in prediction models by improving subpopulation calibration' JAMIA 2021

probe_designs.content: Inspect the 10,000-dim TF-IDF vocabulary for coverage quality. Compute mean note length, vocabulary sparsity, and OOV rate per ethnicity subgroup; compare top informative terms (highest IDF·class-correlation) for mortality-positive vs. negative cases. Verify TF-IDF is fit only on training split (no test leakage). Healthy: balanced sparsity across groups, no spurious tokens (e.g., template artifacts, deidentification placeholders) dominating top-k features. Unhealthy: subgroup-specific note length disparities, dominance of administrative tokens, or stop-word leakage that proxies for ethnicity.
possible_sources: Boag et al. 'What's in a Note? Unpacking Predictive Value in Clinical Note Representations' AMIA 2018
Gehrmann et al. 'Comparing Deep Learning and Concept Extraction Based Methods for Patient Phenotyping from Clinical Narratives' PLOS ONE 2018

probe_designs.content: Track training vs. validation loss/AUROC across epochs and varying training set sizes. Look for over/underfitting: TF-IDF with 10k features over modest ICU cohort can severely overfit. Healthy: small generalization gap (<0.03 AUROC), validation curve plateaus smoothly. Unhealthy: training AUROC ~1.0 with validation <0.80, divergence after few epochs, or non-monotonic validation collapse. Augment with k-fold cross-validation stability (std AUROC < 0.02) and learning curve plots showing whether more data still helps.
possible_sources: Goodfellow et al. 'Deep Learning' Ch.5/7
Rajkomar et al. 'Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records' NPJ Digital Medicine 2018

probe_designs.content: Perturb input clinical notes via synonym substitution (UMLS/clinical word2vec), token deletion, or note truncation and re-extract TF-IDF; measure prediction drift (KL divergence, flip rate). Healthy: predictions stable under semantic-preserving perturbations (<5% flip at decision threshold). Unhealthy: large output swings from removing low-importance tokens, indicating reliance on spurious lexical cues. Specifically test removal of demographic-leakage tokens (e.g., names, locations, language indicators) which can spuriously correlate with ethnicity.
possible_sources: Araujo et al. 'Adversarial Attacks against Medical Deep Learning Systems' arXiv 2018
Wang et al. 'Adversarial Attacks on Medical Machine Learning' Science 2019
Jin et al. 'Is BERT Really Robust?' AAAI 2020

probe_designs.content: Train an auxiliary classifier to predict ethnicity from the 10,000-dim TF-IDF vector alone. If ethnicity is predictable with high accuracy (macro-F1 > 0.5 vs. 0.17 chance for 6 classes), the notes encode demographic signal that the mortality model can exploit even without explicit ethnicity input. Cross-reference: compute mortality model's reliance on ethnicity-correlated tokens via SHAP/LIME. Healthy: weak text-to-ethnicity signal or low overlap between top mortality features and top ethnicity-predictive features. Unhealthy: strong leakage and shared top features, raising proxy-discrimination risk.
possible_sources: Gichoya et al. 'AI recognition of patient race in medical imaging' Lancet Digital Health 2022
Zhang et al. 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings' CHIL 2020

probe_designs.probe_name: Top Feature Plausibility and Clinical Validity
probe_designs.content: Extract the top-k positive/negative coefficients (for linear models) or SHAP/Integrated Gradients (for deep models) over TF-IDF tokens. A clinician should validate that high-importance tokens align with known ICU mortality risk factors (e.g., 'comfort care', 'pressors', 'lactate', 'intubated', 'sepsis') rather than artifacts (e.g., admission unit names, dates, document-format strings). Healthy: clinically coherent top tokens. Unhealthy: dominance of metadata tokens or care-pathway leakage tokens (e.g., 'DNR', 'hospice') that simply restate the outcome and inflate apparent performance.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions' NeurIPS 2017
Caruana et al. 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare' KDD 2015
Ghassemi et al. 'A Review of Challenges and Opportunities in Machine Learning for Health' AMIA Summits 2020

probe_designs.content: ICU in-hospital mortality typically ~10-15% prevalence. Inspect class-balanced loss handling: report PR-AUC (more informative than ROC under imbalance), confusion matrices at clinically chosen operating points (e.g., 90% sensitivity), and decision curve analysis (net benefit). Stratify prevalence by ethnicity to detect Simpson's paradox. Healthy: PR-AUC substantially above prevalence baseline, threshold-tuned recall meets clinical target without precision collapse. Unhealthy: model collapses to majority-class prediction, AUROC misleadingly high while PR-AUC near baseline, or threshold required for fairness varies wildly across subgroups.
possible_sources: Saito & Rehmsmeier 'The Precision-Recall Plot Is More Informative than the ROC Plot' PLOS ONE 2015
Vickers & Elkin 'Decision Curve Analysis' Med Decis Making 2006
Johnson et al. 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database' Scientific Data 2016

probe_designs.content: Evaluate model under temporal split (train on earlier admissions, test on later) and, if available, hold-out ICU unit or hospital. Healthy: AUROC degradation < 0.03 vs. random split, calibration largely preserved. Unhealthy: substantial AUROC drop, especially concentrated in specific ethnicity subgroups, signals overfit to era-specific documentation patterns (vocabulary drift, EHR template changes). Couple with covariate shift detection: KL divergence between train and test TF-IDF distributions, and importance-weighting estimates.
possible_sources: Nestor et al. 'Feature Robustness in Non-stationary Health Records' MLHC 2019
Finlayson et al. 'The Clinician and Dataset Shift in Artificial Intelligence' NEJM 2021
Subbaswamy & Saria 'From development to deployment: dataset shift, causality, and shift-stable models in health AI' Biostatistics 2020

probe_designs.content: Train three model variants: (a) TF-IDF only, (b) ethnicity one-hot only, (c) concatenated TF-IDF + ethnicity. Compare overall AUROC and per-subgroup metrics. Quantify marginal lift from ethnicity input and whether it improves or worsens fairness metrics. Healthy: ethnicity adds minimal accuracy lift and either reduces or leaves fairness gaps unchanged; the model relies on clinical content. Unhealthy: ethnicity contributes meaningful AUROC lift driven by encoding base-rate differences (proxy discrimination), or improves overall metric while widening per-group disparity. Complement with a fairness-through-unawareness vs. fairness-through-awareness comparison and counterfactual ethnicity flip test (predict for the same notes with each ethnicity vector — predictions should be near-invariant).
possible_sources: Kusner et al. 'Counterfactual Fairness' NeurIPS 2017
Dwork et al. 'Fairness Through Awareness' ITCS 2012
Pfohl et al. 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction' JBI 2021
